content.full_text:
Re:
Fern PolrrrcAt- Pnecrrcps C oMMrssroN
-128 J Street r Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA 9581-1-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
July 14,2010
Daniel J. McHugh
Office of the City Attorney
City of Redlands
P.O. Box 3005
35 Cajon Street
Redlands, CA92373
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-111
Dear Mr. McHugh:
This letter responds to your request, on behalf of City Councilmember Mick Gallagher,
for advice concerning a potential conflict of interest. Please note this letter is based on the facts
presented in your request letter. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission")
does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
71.) In addition, nothing in this letter should be constmed to evaluate any conduct that may have
already taken place; the provisions discussed in this letter are intended only to guide future
action. Please note also that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1 We therefore offer no advice on the application, if any, of conflict-of-interest
provisions outside the Act, such as Government Code Section 1090 or common law conflict of
interest.
QUESTION
Is there a potential conflict of interest when a member of the Redlands City Council
participates in "future deliberations and actions" that may affect the terms and/or conditions of
employment of the Councilmember's adult son, and where the Councilmember and his son have
a verbal arrangement whereby the son pays the monthly mortgage payments due on a residence
owned by the Councilmember?
CONCLUSION
Yes. Mr. Gallagher has a potential conflict of interest were he to participate with the City
Council in amending the Memorandum of Understanding (MOU). Mr. Gallagher's arrangement
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections ttl000 through 91014. All statutory
ret'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
CommissionarecontainedinSections lSll0through l8997of Title2of theCalitbrniaCodeof Regulations.All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code olRegulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-10-l I I
Page No. 2
with his son, whereby his son pays the monthly mortgage on a debt owned by Mr. Gallagher and
his spouse, makes his son a potenrially disqualifying iconomic interest.
FACTS
Mick Gallagher, a member of the Redlands City Council, has an adult son who is
employed by the City of Redlands as a firefighter. The City Cor.rncil proposes to take future
"deliberations and actions" concerning possible amendmenis to the "iirtirg MoU between the
City and the Redlands Professional Firefighters Association, of which Mr. -Gallagher,s son is a
member. It is possible that such amendments to the MOU may result in a decrease of the
association members' salaries and/or benefits.
Your letter states that Mr. Gallagher's son presently occupies a residence owned by the
Councilmember and his spouse. There are no written agreements between the son, and
Mr' Gallagher and his spouse, with respect to the son's 6ccupancy of the residence. However,
Mr. Gallagher and his son have a verbal arrangement whereby the son pays the monthly
mortgage payments due on the residence.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits public officials ar any level of state or local
government from making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to Llse his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has ,.ufon to know he has a
financial interest- (Section 87100; Regulation 18700.) An official has a,.financial interest,, in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effeit on one or more of the official's economic interests.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) To determine if an individual has a disqualifying
financial interest under the Act, the Commission applies the following eight step analysis.
Step One: Is the individual a..public official,,?
The Act's conflict ofinterest provisions apply only to "public officials.,, (Section
87100') Defined in Section 82048(a), "public officiat"..on, every member, officer, employee,
or consultant of a state or local govemment agency. As a member of tn" Redlands City Council,
Mr' Gallagher is a "public official" for purpoJes oi the Act. Thus, he is subject to the Act,s
conflict of interest provisions.
Step Two: Is the public official making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
ln determining whether a conflict of interest may be present, a govemmental decision
needs to be identified, as well as the public official's o.iion, related toihot decision. A public
offlcial "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the ar-rthority of his or


File No. A-10-111
Page No.3
her office or position, votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any collrse of action, enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency, or determines not to act. (Regulation lg7OZ.l.)
A public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within
the authority of his or her position, the official negotiates, without significant substantive review,
with a governmental entity or private person regaiding a govemmental decision, or advises or
makes recommendations to the decision maker iitt e. Oire"tly or without significant intervening
substantive review, including conducting research or an investigation, or pr"eparing or presentiig
a report, analysis or opinion. (Regulation Lg7O2.Z(b)(l) and (21.)
A public official is "attempting to influence a governmental decision" through his or her
official position if the official contacts or appears before, or otherwise attempts to iifluence any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of the agency of which he or she is employed.
(Regulation 18702.3-) Further, a public official iJattempting to influence rhe decision of an
agency of which he or she is not employed when purporting to act on behalf of, or as the
representative of, his or her agency in the ra*e *orrrrir as described above. (Regulation
18702.3(b).)
You ask whether Mr. Gallagher may participate in "deliberations and actions,, and
"negotiations" concerning an agreement between the city and a professional association. This
constitutes "making," "participating in making," or "attemptingio influence,, a govemmental
decision.2
Step Three: Does the pubtic official have one of the five types of economic interests?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions identify five general categories of economic
interests as enumerated in Regulations 18703.1 through 1870J.5. These frr" g"n"rol categories
are: business entities, real property, sources of incomJ, sources of gifts, and pfrsonal finances.
1' A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more, or if the official is a diiector, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position in management. (Section 87103(a) and (d); i.egulation
18703.1(a) and (b).)
2. A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103@);Regulation lg713.Z.)
I If a public otficial's ottlce is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 fllers" include city council members) and he
or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( l) immediately prior
to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic iiterest involved in the decision as well as
details of the economic interest. as discussed in Regulation 18702.5(bX IXB). on rhe record of the meeting; (2)
recu'sehimselforherselt:and(3)leavetheroomt'.,rthedurationofineaiscrssionand/orvoteontheitem. 
For
closed sessions' consent calendars, absences and .speaking as a member of the public regarding persgnal interests.
special rules fbund in Regulation 1g702.5, subdivi.sions f c) ana (d) apply. (Section tt7l05.)


File No. A-10-11I
Page No.4
3' A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to 5500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $420 or more within twelve months prior to the dec[ion. (Section g7103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
5. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and those of
his or her immediate family. A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic
interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, assets, income, or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family increasing oi decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation
r8703.5.)
For Mr. Gallagher, "sources of income," and "personal finances" are the most likely
sources of a potential conflict of interest. For "sourcei of income" economic interests,
Regulation 18703.3 states that a public official has an economic interest in any person from
whom he or she has-received or been promised income, aggregating $500 doliars or more within
12 months prior to the time when the relevant government,al Oecision is made. Section g2030(a)
defines "income" to include any "payment received" for "rent" or "forgiveness or payment of
indebtedness received by the filer.,,
Because the_monthly mortgage payments on the residence in question are debts of the
Councilmember and,his spouse, paym.ntt by the son on these debts aie considered a,.payment
of indebtedness" and thus "income" under Section 82020(a). Further, the facts presented in your
letter indicate that the payments made by the son on this debt appear to be a kind of ..rent,,
payment alrangement between the Councilmember and his ro.r. 
-Ass.,ming 
these paymenB have
totaled $500 or more over any 12 month period prior to any govemmental decision made or
participated in by the Councilmember under Step Three, Mr. Gallagher's son is considered a
"source of income" economic interest.
As for an economic interest in "personal finances," a public official always has this
interest in his or her personal finances as well as those olhis or her immediate ffi;: 
ii".tio.,
87103') If any governmental decision made by Mr. Gallagher results in a change of his son,s
salary and/or benefits, and this in turn results in any increa-se or decrease in the amollnt his son is
able to pay under their arrangement, this might have an effect on the personal finances of Mr.
Gallagher and./or his spouse considering that the son's payments are ,.income,, to both.
I Section 1J2029 dc'fines "immediate tanrily" to inclucle rhe public oftlcial's spogse and dependent chil6ren
we assume that NIr. Gallagher's s.n is not included in this detlnition.


File No. A-10-111
Page No. 5
Step Four: Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision?
To determine whether a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect
on an economic interest is material or not, you tirst must determine whether the interest is
directly or indirectly involved. (Regulation lg70a(a).)
Economic interests in "sources of income" arc directly involved in a governmental
decision before the official's agency when that interest, eithei directly or thrJugh an agent,
initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made or is a po.ty nu*"d in, or is the
subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation 18704.1.) Further, the intiresiis a subject of the
proceeding if the decision involves the "renewal, approval (or) denial," of any ..entitlement to or
contract with" the interest. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) The economic interest will be deemed
indirectly involved in the govemmental decision if found not to be directly involved. (Reg.
18704(a).)
In the past, we have advised that because union contracts generally affect all city
employees, or at least an entire class of city employees, the indiviiual city employ"", *ho -oy
be economic interests of a member of the city council are not directly inrtlveA in the contract
process. (Thiltgen Advice Letter, No. I-03-070.) The DeBerry Advice Letter, No. t-00-lgg,
advised that city employees who rented from a city Councilmimber were .,sources of income,,
economic interests of that Councilmember, but would only be indirectly involved in the ..contract
process" between the city council and the employees' union. Similarlyfor Mr. Gallagher,
presuming the MOU would affect his son's "entire class" of city empllyees (firemen), his son
would be indirectly involved in the council's deliberations, actions, or negotiations.
Economic interests in personal finances are directly involved when there is any financial
effect on the public official's finances or those of his or her immediate family. (Regulation
187 04.5, emphasis added.)
Steps Five and Six: Will the governmental decision have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the public official's economic interest?
If Mr' Gallagher's son is found to be indirectly involved, Regulation 1g705.3(bX3)
applies. Regulation 18705.3(b)(3) states that the effeit of a decision is material if either of the
following apply:
(A) 
The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property)
by $ 1000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title2, california code of
Regulations, secrions I 8705.2(b).


File No. A-10-l1l
Page No. 6
Applying the above language, if an amendment to the MOU results in a $1000 change to
the son's income or other assets, the financial effect will be material. your letter has presen-ted
no facts to determine if subdivisions (bX3)(B) or (b)(3XC) may apply. This is somerhing
Mr. Gallagher will have to consider when the appropriate time c*"r.
For the interest in "personal finances," reasonably foreseeable financial effects on a
public official's or his or her immediate family's p"rronul finances are deemed material if the
effect is at least $250 in any twelve month period. (Regulation 18705.5(a).) If an amendment to
the MoU results in a reduction or an increase of the soi's salary, and this'reduces or enhances
the son's ability to make the payments by $250 or more over a iwelve month period, the effect
will be material.a
A material financial effect on any economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it is
"substantially likely" that the applicable materiality standard will be n1"i o, a result of the
govemmental decision- (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered
reasonably foreseeable, but sttch an effect must be more than a mere possibility. (tn re Tltorner
(1975) I FPPC ops. 198.) Your letter does not provide enough facts to determine whether it is
"substantially likely" that any amendment to the MOU will nieet any of these materiality
standards. Mr. Gallagher will have to decide for himself whether it is "substantially likely" an
amendment will increase or decrease his son's income or other assets by $1000, oriirrrituity
whether it is "substantially likely" such amendment would affect his oriis spouse,s own income
by $zso. We provide the applicable language above to help guide the Councilmember,s
decision-making.
Steps Seven and Eight: The Public Generally Exception and Legally Required
Participation
Even if Steps one through Six suggest a disqualifying conflict of interest, the economic
interest is not disqualifying if the official can establirt, tt ot the governmental decision will affect
their interest in a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will
affect the public generally. (Regulation 1s707(a).) This analysis is accomplished through a
four-step process set forth in Regulations lg707.l through rg707.g.
For both of Mr. Gallagher's identified economic interests, Regulation lg707.l(b)(l)(A)
states that for the "public generally" exception to apply, either ten percent or more of the
population of the city or 5,000 individuals within ttri city must be affected by an amendment to
the MoU similarly to the way in which Mr. Gallagher's interest in "personoifinon""s,, and his
son are affected. Thus, the Redlands Professional Firefighters AssoCiation must be comprised of
ten percent of the population of the city, or have at least 5,000 members who would be affected
in the same way his son is affected.s Also, either ten percent of the population or 5,000
individuals will have to have their income affected in a way similar to vtr. Gallagher and his
{ Again' we are considering these monthly payments as "income" to Mr. Gallagher and his spouse.
s These members must also be residents of the City of Redlands.


File No. A-10-l I I
Page No. 7
spollse. We do not have any facts suggesting these criteria are met, and indeed we doubt that
they are.
Another exception, "legally required participation," may apply if Mr. Gallagher can
establish that he is legally required to make oi partiiipate in mating tir" gor"*mental decision.
If so, he may take part in such decision notwithstanding his disqualifying interest as long as he
complies with the required reporting requirements.6 1R-egulation tszb8(-o).) ro claim this
exception, Mr. Gallagher must establish that "there exists no alternative source of decision
consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision.,, (Regulation
18708(a).) It should be noted that this regulation is "construed-narrowly,,, and would not allow
Mr. Gallagher to vote on the matter "if a quorum can be convened" of Council members who are
not disqualified even if a vote is needed "to break a tie."7 (Regulation lg70g(c).) Again, your
letter does not present facts that suggest this exception may apply.
without further information regarding the substance of any potential amendments to the
Mou' we are unable to render specific advice. However, from thl -li-it"d 
facts presented in
your letter, if an amendment to the MoU results in an increase or decrease of the son,s income
or assets by $1000, it is likely that Mr. Gallagher has a disqualifying conflict of interest. We
trust that the discussion above will help you identify the areas and circumstances under which a
conflict might arise with respect to this situation. ti in the future more specific facts come to
light, you may submit those facts and request further advice.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me
at (916) 322-s66O.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Zache:ry Lopes
Legal Intem
D
ZL:jgl
6 These reporting requirements are tbund in subdivision (b) of Regulation l g70g.
' Fclr purposes.of this provision' a "quorum" shall constitute the minimum number of members required to
conduct business and when the vote of a supeimajority is required to nJc,p, an item. the..quorum.. shall be that
minimum nunrber of members needed tor tirat adoptitin. rReguration r g70ri(d).)

a verbal arrangement whereby the son pays the monthly mortgage payments due on a residence
owned by the Councilmember?

Council in amending the Memorandum of Understanding (MOU). Mr. Gallagher's arrangement

with his son, whereby his son pays the monthly mortgage on a debt owned by Mr. Gallagher and
his spouse, makes his son a potenrially disqualifying iconomic interest.

financial effect on the public official's economic interest?
If Mr' Gallagher's son is found to be indirectly involved, Regulation 1g705.3(bX3)
applies. Regulation 18705.3(b)(3) states that the effeit of a decision is material if either of the
following apply:
(A) 
The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or
other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property)
by $ 1000 or more; or
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under Title2, california code of
Regulations, secrions I 8705.2(b).


File No. A-10-l1l
Applying the above language, if an amendment to the MOU results in a $1000 change to
the son's income or other assets, the financial effect will be material. your letter has presen-ted
no facts to determine if subdivisions (bX3)(B) or (b)(3XC) may apply. This is somerhing
Mr. Gallagher will have to consider when the appropriate time c*"r.
For the interest in "personal finances," reasonably foreseeable financial effects on a
public official's or his or her immediate family's p"rronul finances are deemed material if the
effect is at least $250 in any twelve month period. (Regulation 18705.5(a).) If an amendment to
the MoU results in a reduction or an increase of the soi's salary, and this'reduces or enhances
the son's ability to make the payments by $250 or more over a iwelve month period, the effect
will be material.a
A material financial effect on any economic interest is reasonably foreseeable if it is
"substantially likely" that the applicable materiality standard will be n1"i o, a result of the
govemmental decision- (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered
reasonably foreseeable, but sttch an effect must be more than a mere possibility. (tn re Tltorner
(1975) I FPPC ops. 198.) Your letter does not provide enough facts to determine whether it is
"substantially likely" that any amendment to the MOU will nieet any of these materiality
standards. Mr. Gallagher will have to decide for himself whether it is "substantially likely" an
amendment will increase or decrease his son's income or other assets by $1000, oriirrrituity
whether it is "substantially likely" such amendment would affect his oriis spouse,s own income
by $zso. We provide the applicable language above to help guide the Councilmember,s
decision-making.
Steps Seven and Eight: The Public Generally Exception and Legally Required
Participation
Even if Steps one through Six suggest a disqualifying conflict of interest, the economic
interest is not disqualifying if the official can establirt, tt ot the governmental decision will affect
their interest in a manner which is indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will
affect the public generally. (Regulation 1s707(a).) This analysis is accomplished through a
four-step process set forth in Regulations lg707.l through rg707.g.
For both of Mr. Gallagher's identified economic interests, Regulation lg707.l(b)(l)(A)
states that for the "public generally" exception to apply, either ten percent or more of the
population of the city or 5,000 individuals within ttri city must be affected by an amendment to
the MoU similarly to the way in which Mr. Gallagher's interest in "personoifinon""s,, and his
son are affected. Thus, the Redlands Professional Firefighters AssoCiation must be comprised of
ten percent of the population of the city, or have at least 5,000 members who would be affected
in the same way his son is affected.s Also, either ten percent of the population or 5,000
individuals will have to have their income affected in a way similar to vtr. Gallagher and his
{ Again' we are considering these monthly payments as "income" to Mr. Gallagher and his spouse.
s These members must also be residents of the City of Redlands.


File No. A-10-l I I
spollse. We do not have any facts suggesting these criteria are met, and indeed we doubt that
they are.
Another exception, "legally required participation," may apply if Mr. Gallagher can
establish that he is legally required to make oi partiiipate in mating tir" gor"*mental decision.
If so, he may take part in such decision notwithstanding his disqualifying interest as long as he
complies with the required reporting requirements.6 1R-egulation tszb8(-o).) ro claim this
exception, Mr. Gallagher must establish that "there exists no alternative source of decision
consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision.,, (Regulation
18708(a).) It should be noted that this regulation is "construed-narrowly,,, and would not allow
Mr. Gallagher to vote on the matter "if a quorum can be convened" of Council members who are
not disqualified even if a vote is needed "to break a tie."7 (Regulation lg70g(c).) Again, your
letter does not present facts that suggest this exception may apply.
without further information regarding the substance of any potential amendments to the
Mou' we are unable to render specific advice. However, from thl -li-it"d 
facts presented in
your letter, if an amendment to the MoU results in an increase or decrease of the son,s income
or assets by $1000, it is likely that Mr. Gallagher has a disqualifying conflict of interest. We
trust that the discussion above will help you identify the areas and circumstances under which a
conflict might arise with respect to this situation. ti in the future more specific facts come to
light, you may submit those facts and request further advice.

QUESTION: Is there a potential conflict of interest when a member of the Redlands City Council
participates in "future deliberations and actions" that may affect the terms and/or conditions of
employment of the Councilmember's adult son, and where the Councilmember and his son have
a verbal arrangement whereby the son pays the monthly mortgage payments due on a residence
owned by the Councilmember?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Mr. Gallagher has a potential conflict of interest were he to participate with the City
Council in amending the Memorandum of Understanding (MOU). Mr. Gallagher's arrangement

McHugh Office of the City Attorney City of Redlands P.O. Box 3005 35 Cajon Street Redlands, CA92373 Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-111 Dear Mr. McHugh: This letter responds to your request, on behalf of City Councilmember Mick Gallagher, for advice concerning a potential conflict of interest. Please note this letter is based on the facts presented in your request letter. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) In addition, nothing in this letter should be constmed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place; the provisions discussed in this letter are intended only to guide future action. Please note also that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We therefore offer no advice on the application, if any, of conflict-of-interest provisions outside the Act, such as Government Code Section 1090 or common law conflict of interest. QUESTION Is there a potential conflict of interest when a member of the Redlands City Council participates in "future deliberations and actions" that may affect the terms and/or conditions of employment of the Councilmember's adult son, and where the Councilmember and his son have a verbal arrangement whereby the son pays the monthly mortgage payments due on a residence owned by the Councilmember? CONCLUSION Yes. Mr. Gallagher has a potential conflict of interest were he to participate with the City Council in amending the Memorandum of Understanding (MOU).
